bibliographic.citation: Killis Campbell, ed., The Seven Sages of Rome (Boston, New York, Chicago, and London: Ginn & Company, 1907), Albion Series.
bibliographic.title: The Seven Sages of Rome

entities.label: Killis Campbell's 1907 edition of The Seven Sages of Rome
notes: Campbell selects C as base because R is incomplete and C is more archaic in spelling and more accurate in minor respects, despite C's later hand.

C is followed except for about twenty stated scribal-error emendations. Campbell supplies punctuation, paragraphing, normalized capitalization, abbreviation expansion, modern word division, lineation, and bracketed manuscript folio and story labels; emendations are bracketed and manuscript readings footnoted.
R variants are generally printed diplomatically except that abbreviations are expanded.

rights.basis: The work was published in the United States in 1907, before 1 January 1931, the current public-domain threshold stated by U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A.
rights.component: 1907 editorial text, introduction, notes, glossary, and typographical arrangement

rights.basis: Campbell died in 1937; current UK guidance gives written works a usual term of life plus seventy years and published-edition layout twenty-five years from publication, both elapsed.
rights.component: Campbell's editorial writing and the edition's typographical arrangement

entities.label: Henry Weber's Metrical Romances, volume III
notes: Weber's Proces is not a unitary witness transcription: Auchinleck supplies the central fragment, with Cotton used for the opening and ending. Campbell cites the composite text as W.
Campbell maps the Cotton-supplied portions to C lines 1–134 and 3108–4328; Weber's own lineation and statement describe the transition through the Auchinleck fragment.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1810 and Henry Weber died in 1816; both the U.S. publication-based maximum and UK life-plus-seventy and layout terms have elapsed.

reviewNotes: The intellectual subject is the anonymous Northern redaction, not the full Seven Sages tradition, the broader Middle English Version A or Y Group, either complete manuscript miscellany, Campbell's apparatus, Weber's composite text, or either electronic representation.

Campbell concludes that C and R independently descend from a lost common exemplar cr; neither witness is modeled as copied from the other, and reconstructed cr and y are not modeled as extant entities.

The XML's 4,328 L elements comprise 4,326 base-text lines and two R-only lines nested in a footnote. Campbell/Cotton base lines 1832 and 3533 are absent, so the matching total is accidental rather than proof of completeness.

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00033 XML represents the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item keyed from Killis Campbell's 1907 edition The Seven Sages of Rome. Campbell edited the complete 4,328-line Northern Text C from British Library Cotton MS Galba E IX, ff. 25v–48v, and supplied variants from the incomplete Northern Text R in Bodleian Library MS Rawl. poet. 175, ff. 109r–131v. He also compared Weber's 1810 composite text, whose central fragment comes from the parallel Southern/Y witness National Library of Scotland Adv.MS.19.2.1, ff. 85r–99v, while Cotton supplies its opening and ending. Complete public scans of Campbell and Weber and the complete 701-image public Auchinleck project inventory are cached; the latter is only partial coverage of the physical manuscript. No publicly obtainable complete facsimile of Cotton Galba E IX or Rawl. poet. 175 was verified on 2026-07-12, so exact mediated whole-object imaging routes and reuse distinctions are recorded.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Composed by about 1350, perhaps in the first half of the fourteenth century; attested in a mid- to late-fourteenth-century witness; portions first printed in 1810 and the complete Northern text in 1907
catalogSummary.region: Middle English work with a Northern English dialect association; exact place of composition is unknown; frame narrative set in Rome
catalogSummary.title: The Seven Sages of Rome (Northern Version)

catalogingSubject.label: The Seven Sages of Rome (Northern Version)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous Northern Middle English verse redaction of The Seven Sages of Rome, represented by Text C in British Library Cotton MS Galba E IX and the incomplete Text R in Bodleian Library MS Rawl. poet. 175. It excludes the wider multilingual Seven Sages / Book of Sindbad tradition, the broader Middle English Version A and Y Group, other Middle English redactions, the complete manuscript miscellanies, Campbell's 1907 edition and apparatus, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, and digital facsimiles as distinct layers.

contentParts.label: Opening frame narrative
contentParts.summary: The emperor, prince, seven sages, education, remarriage, astrological danger, return to court, accusation, and first threatened execution through the demand for the first delaying tale.

contentParts.label: Fifteen prologue-and-story pairs and frame conclusion
notes: The encoded metric is two lines short of Campbell's 3,736-line scholarly range because base-text lines 1832 and 3533 are absent; the two nested R-only lines are apparatus and are not counted here.
contentParts.summary: Fifteen alternating speeches and embedded stories, ending with the prince's Vaticinium story and the exposure, judgment, and execution of the Empress.

assertion.notes: The terminus is inferred from the earliest surviving witness. Campbell's narrower proposal concerns a reconstructed exemplar and is not normalized as a secure composition interval.

dateStatements.display: The Northern redaction existed by about 1350; Campbell tentatively proposed the first quarter of the fourteenth century for the lost common exemplar behind C and R
Bodleian Library MS Rawl. poet. 175, the earlier incomplete witness R, is dated to the second half of the fourteenth century (1350–1400)
Henry Weber printed a composite Northern/Southern text in 1810, including the Cotton opening and ending

scope.description: Northern Text C opening and ending within Weber's composite Proces of the Seuyn Sages

dateStatements.display: Campbell published the complete Northern Text C with R variants in 1907

extent.description: Campbell's complete base text is lineated 1–4,328. The XML has 31 direct work LG elements containing 4,326 base-text L elements: 592 opening-frame lines and 3,734 lines across thirty alternating prologue/story divisions. A thirty-second LG, nested in a footnote after Campbell line 2364, contains two Rawlinson-only added lines. Thus count(//L)=4,328 does not mean the complete 4,328-line base text is present: Campbell/Cotton lines 1832 and 3533 are omitted. The XML also has 30 DIV2, 32 HEAD, 149 PB, 958 MILESTONE, and 3,509 NOTE elements. These are representation metrics, not a claim that every editorial division is authorial.

formStatements.description: A Middle English verse romance in predominantly octosyllabic rhyming couplets, lineated by Campbell to 4,328 lines.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English and Latin occur in Campbell's editorial apparatus and labels, not as intrinsic languages of the medieval work.
Work, witnesses, scholarly editions, electronic encoding, repository artifact, facsimiles, and rights are modeled as separate layers.
C and R are independent descendants of a reconstructed lost common exemplar in Campbell's analysis; neither is asserted to have been copied from the other.
The Auchinleck manuscript is a parallel Southern/Y witness used by Weber's composite edition, not a direct witness of the Northern redaction.

next_steps: Consult post-Campbell stemmatic and dialect studies before narrowing the date below the terminus supplied by R.
Treat Campbell's first-quarter-fourteenth-century and York-neighborhood suggestions as tentative.
Do not create extant-source lineage entities for reconstructed cr or y.
openQuestions.question: When and where was the Northern redaction composed, and how exactly does it relate to the reconstructed lost C/R exemplar cr and the broader Y ancestor?

next_steps: Collate the source XML systematically against Campbell's pp. 1–145 and the U-M displayed text.
Ask U-M whether the omissions are known keying defects or reflect an earlier source file.
Keep representation metrics separated from Campbell's 4,328-line scholarly extent.
openQuestions.question: Why does the U-M / repository encoding omit Campbell/Cotton base-text lines 1832 and 3533 while including two R-only lines as nested L elements in an apparatus note?

next_steps: Request item-level production metadata and any source package from U-M.
Keep the 2016 TEMPHEAD date scoped to review rather than creation.
Do not infer OCR versus manual transcription from the current XML alone.
openQuestions.question: What initial keying method, correction history, and source-file terms apply to the U-M CME00033 encoding before its documented 2016 review?

assertion.notes: Northern is secure as the redactional and linguistic classification; exact composition at York is not established.

notes: Rome is the frame narrative's court setting; embedded stories have varied or generic settings.

assertion.notes: The work is an anonymous Northern redaction within the Middle English Version A / Y tradition. A later attribution to Richard Rolle is unsupported.

notes: Campbell based the text on Cotton Galba E IX, collated Rawlinson poet. 175, and cited Weber as W; his supplied punctuation, paragraphing, capitalization, word division, expansions, and emendations belong to the edition layer.

subjects.label: False accusation, delayed execution, and rash judgment

assertion.notes: The classification describes a sustained rhetorical pattern while allowing for the tales' competing speakers and unreliable counsel.

subjects.label: Education, astrological foreknowledge, and enforced silence

summaries.value: A Roman emperor sends his son to seven sages for education. On the prince's return, astrological foreknowledge requires seven days of silence; his stepmother propositions him and, when refused, falsely accuses him. The emperor repeatedly orders the prince's execution, while the Empress and sages alternate cautionary tales until the prince can speak, expose the plot, and tell his own final tale. The Northern redaction is distinguished by its C/R textual tradition and includes a distinctive witchcraft episode.

After a 592-line opening frame, the XML alternates fifteen prologues and fifteen story divisions: Arbor, Canis, Aper, Medicus, Gaza, Puteus, Senescalcus, Tentamina, Virgilius, Avis, Sapientes, Vidua, Roma, Inclusa, and Vaticinium, with the frame conclusion in the final division.

Campbell's printed story names, folio markers, lineation, punctuation, paragraphing, and editorial italics are navigational or editorial features, not all recoverable authorial divisions. The XML's total of 4,328 L elements is accidentally equal to Campbell's lineation: only 4,326 are base-text lines, while two are Rawlinson-only variant lines nested in a footnote and two Campbell/Cotton lines are absent.

assertion.notes: The parenthetical qualifier distinguishes this redaction from the Midland and Southern versions; it is not a medieval rubric.

titles.value: The Seven Sages of Rome (Northern Version)

notes: The XML records that R has spelling variants in the heading.
titles.value: Here Bigyns þe Proces of þe Seuyn Sages
Lordynges þat here likes to dwell

notes: Campbell's final verse line is followed by the prose closer AMEN.

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Composed by about 1350, perhaps in the first half of the fourteenth century; attested in a mid- to late-fourteenth-century witness; portions first printed in 1810 and the complete Northern text in 1907
work.displayTitle: The Seven Sages of Rome (Northern Version)
work.preferredTitle: The Seven Sages of Rome (Northern Version)
work.regionDisplay: Middle English work with a Northern English dialect association; exact place of composition is unknown; frame narrative set in Rome
